insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles verified purchase confirmation at roughly $0.10 per receipt — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't a pricing war, it's a different category of evidence.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the industry has no cross-site, cross-ecosystem purchase signal at scale — verified receipt tokens are the only identity-free alternative currently producing real GMV data ($227K confirmed).

insights.topic: Platform silo as structural blind spot
insights.insight: Amazon, Walmart, and Kroger each see only their own purchase data; the protocol's cross-ecosystem token pool is the first verified commerce signal that crosses retailer walls without requiring customer identity to be shared.

9 non-metro state designations are active across 17 CBSAs, with Walmart, Kroger, and Dollar General receipts dominating — the geographic spread is tracking social graphs, not ad spend, which means this audience is not reachable through standard metro-weighted targeting models.

insights.topic: AI and SaaS receipt cohort emerging
insights.insight: Vendors including Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gamma are already appearing in the DKIM receipt pipeline, signaling that a distinct AI-native buyer cohort is submitting purchase data — a segment most brand measurement systems have no visibility into.

The competitive dynamic here mirrors data network effects: more verified receipts increase query value, which increases reward incentive, which draws more receipts — brands that access or contribute to the dataset early avoid paying infrastructure-level prices later.
